Facts.text: Anthemius is elevated to Emperor of the Western Roman Empire by Leo I
Pilgrims under Bishop Gunther of Bamberg reach Jerusalem
The Fourth Crusade occupies and plunders Constantinople
Queen Blanche of Castile & Earl Raymond VII of Toulouse sign peace
Cuenca is founded in Ecuador
England adopts the Union Flag, replaced in 1801 by current Union Flag, also known as the Union Jack
University of Harderwijk opens in the Netherlands
Ordinance of Union between England and Scotland passed by the Council of State
Dutch State-Gen signs peace with France: Netherlands loses Orange Princedom
British parliament repeals the Townshend Revenue Acts, which had fueled opposition to British rule in colonial America
Halifax resolution for independence adopted by North Carolina
Philadelphia's Free African Society forms
First US colonists on the Pacific coast arrive at Cape Disappointment, Washington
Alexander Ypsilantis is declared leader of Filiki Eteria, a secret organization founded in Odessa to overthrow Ottoman rule of Greece
Weber's opera "Oberon" premieres in London
Texan envoys sign Treaty of Annexation with the United States
1st US billiards championship, Michael J Phelan wins in Detroit
Hibernia Savings & Loan Society of San Francisco incorporates
James Andrews steals Confederate train (General) at Kennesaw, Georgia
Union troops occupy Fort Pulaski, Georgia
-14] Gunboat battle at Bayou Teche, Louisiana
Battle of Blair's Landing, Louisiana
North Carolina legislature passes anti-Ku Klux Klan Law
Canada's parliament passes the Indian Act to administer status and lands of First Nations People - primarily to assimilate them
British annex Transvaal, South Africa
Catcher's mask 1st used in a baseball game
French troops under lt-colonel Borgnis-Desbordes occupy Bamako Senegal
George C Blickensderfer patents portable typewriter

"Massacre of Hoornkrans": Curt von François, colonial Governor of German South West Africa (now Nambia), leads attack by 225 Schutztruppe soldiers on Nama leader Hendrik Witbooi's headquarters at Hoornkrans; shelling of the village causes tremendous civilian casualties. Witbooi escapes and wages several months of guerrilla warfare against the German forces.
British & Belgian secret accord on dividing Central Africa
Stamasia Portrisi is 1st woman to win a marathon (5:30 in Athens)
US Army transfers Yerba Buena Island in San Francisco Bay to Navy
The US Congress passes the Foraker Act, establishing Puerto Rico as an unincorporated territory (effective 1 May)
French Dufaux brothers test helicopter
Hippodrome arena opens (NYC)
Belgium government of Paul Smet de Naeyer resigns
In Switzerland, parliament passes a new army bill reorganizing the nation's forces into a standing militia, with training required for all males
Fire at the Boston Blacking Company (producer of leather dyes), spreads by high wind, kills 19 and makes 17,000 homeless in Chelsea, Massachusetts
Philadelphia's Shibe Park (later Connie Mack Stadium), baseball's first steel and concrete stadium, opens; Athletics win 8-1 against Boston Red Sox
First non-stop London-Paris flight by Pierre Prier in 3 hours and 56 minutes
Irish nationalist activist and poet Roger Casement boards submarine U-19 at Wilmshaven, Germany, bound for a rendezvous with the Aud at Tralee
Bijou Theater opens at 222 W 45th Street, NYC; demolished in 1982
Domenico Scarlatti & Jeab Cocteaus ballet premieres in Rome
British Parliament passes a 48-hour work week with minimum wages
WLS-AM in Chicago begins radio transmissions
Dutch Catholic Radio Broadcast (KRO) forms
4th Test Cricket WI v England ends in a draw after nine days
Wilfred Rhodes ends Test Cricket career aged 52 years 165 days
Spanish voters reject the monarchy
Emmanuel Chabriers & Balanchines ballet premieres in Monte Carlo
US Navy commissions Air Station Sunnyvale (later renamed NAS Moffatt Field) in Santa Clara county, California (at the southern end of San Francisco Bay)
Second highest ever wind speed of 372 km/h (231 mph) recorded on Mt Washington
US Auto-Lite Strike begins, culminating in a five-day melee between Ohio National Guard troops and 6,000 strikers and picketers
First flight of the Bristol Blenheim
Germany prohibits publishing of works by "not-Aryan" writers
Royal Proclamation sets design of Canada's new Jubilee Silver Dollar
Engineer Frank Whittle ground-tests the first jet engine designed to power an aircraft in Rugby, England
First US law requiring medical tests for marriage licenses in New York
Stanley Cup Final, Chicago Stadium, Chicago, IL: Chicago Black Hawks beat Toronto Maple Leafs, 4-1 for a 3-1 series win; only team to win Cup with losing regular season record
Italy annexes Albania
NFL cuts clipping penalty from 25 yards to 15 yards
Stanley Cup Final, Olympia Stadium, Detroit, MI: Boston Bruins defeat Detroit Red Wings, 3-1 for a 4-0 series sweep
Japan kills about 400 Filipino officers in Bataan
Allies conquer Soussa, North-Africa
Dutch Catholic University Nijmegen closed
Canadian troops liberate Nazi concentration camp Westerbork, Netherlands
WWII: British Royal Navy captures German U-boat U-1024  in the Irish; it sinks while being towed the next day
Syria gains independence from France
Israeli Knesset officially designates April 13 as Holocaust Day
Salaheddine Baccouche forms Tunisian government
Keizo Yamada runs fastest marathon to date, at Boston
KFDX TV channel 3 in Wichita Falls, TX (NBC) begins broadcasting
Atlantic Records releases "Big" Joe Turner single "Shake, Rattle & Roll"; tops the R&B charts, and reaches #22 on the pop charts, before being covered by Bill Haley
Belgian Van Houtte government resigns
1st game in KC, KC A's beat Detroit Tigers, 6-2
S.W.R.D. Bandaranaike's government takes office in Ceylon (now Sri Lanka)
Jim Spalding set a 2,088 pin nine-game bowling record
USSR performs atmospheric nuclear test
Flemish Open air museum opens in Bokrijk
13th Tony Awards: "J.B." (play) & "Redhead" (musical) win
France Observator reports torture practice by French army in Algeria
San Mateo County withdraws from BART (San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit) district
Beatles third single, "From Me To You" is released in UK
Police in Birmingham, Alabama, use dogs & cattle prods on peaceful demonstrators
1st regular season MLB game at Houston Astrodome; visiting Philadelphia Phillies beat Astros 2-0
1st B-52 bombing on North Vietnam
Atlanta-Fulton County Stadium opens; Pirates beat Braves 3-2
Rocker Jan Berry crashes his Corvette into a parked truck, an accident which leaves him with long-lasting health issues
Simon & Garfunkel releases "Boxer"
France recognizes North Vietnam
Sudan adopts constitution
Swaziland suspends constitution
Six Catholic civilians are killed in a Ulster Volunteer Force gun and grenade attack on Strand Bar in Belfast, North Ireland
India set 403 to win by WI They get them, 6 wkts 7 overs spare
Gyorgy Ligeti's opera "Le Grand Macabre" premieres in Stockholm
Soyuz 33 returns to Earth
BCMA, Black Consciousness Movement of Azania, forms
Milwaukee beats Boston Red Sox, 18-1 (Cooper & Money hit grand slams)
Samuel Doe takes control of Liberia in a coup d'etat, killing President William Talbert and 27 others, ending over 130 years of national democratic presidential succession
US Olympic Committee endorses a boycott of the Moscow summer Olympics
Maiden voyage Space Transit System-space shuttle Columbia launched
3 CBS employees shot to death in NYC parking lot
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
16th Shuttle Mission (51D)-Discovery 4 launched-with Senator Jake Garn
20,000 mine workers protest closing of Hasselt Belgium mines
Ahmed Salah wins 2nd World Cup marathon (2:10:55)
CBS' premiere of fact-based World War II drama "Escape from Sobibor"
Texaco files for bankruptcy
Devils 4-2 over Islanders-Devils lead 3-2 in 1st round
Harvard University patents a genetically engineered mouse (first for animal life)
1st meeting of East German democratically elected parliament, acknowledges responsibility for the Holocaust and asks for forgiveness
Greyhound Bus hires new drivers to replace strikers
H. J. Heinz, Chicken of the Sea and Bumble Bee Seafood say they wouldn't buy tuna caught in nets that trap dolphins
2,500th episode of Entertainment Tonight airs
Amantes (Lovers: A True Story), directed by Vicente Aranda and starring Victoria Abril and Jorge Sanz, premieres in Spain
Nepalese Congress party wins general elections
US announces closing of 31 major US military bases
2nd lowest NBA scoring game - Detroit Pistons 72, NY Knicks 61
Earthquake rocks Germany
Euro Disney (Disneyland Paris) opens in Marne-la-Vallee, France
Lynn Gunther of California threatens to blow herself up in front of UN
MLB Boston Red Sox pitcher Matt Young no-hits Cleveland Indians, but loses 2-1
NY Mets lose 1st 3 home games for 1st time since 1962
Trump Shuttle becomes US Air Shuttle
American Jeff Rouse swims world record 100m backstroke (51.43 sec) at Sheffield, England
Canter & Siegel post the first commercial mass Usenet spam.
An earthquake in Slovenia, measuring 5.6 on the Richter scale occurs near the town of Bovec.
Palestinian suicide bomber (female) kills 7 and injures 104 (among them 9 Arabs) at the Mahane Yehuda Market in Jerusalem.
U.S. Navy rescues captain Richard Phillips, killing three pirates and capturing a fourth
A train derailed near Merano, Italy, after running into a landslide, causing nine deaths and injuring 28 people.
A ceasefire in the 2011-2012 Syrian uprising comes into effect
Bodleian, Oxford University and Vatican libraries announce over 1.5 million pages of ancient texts will be made available across the internet
Civilian rule in Mali is returned after Dioncounda Traore is sworn in as interim president
11 people are killed and 30 are injured in mosque attacks across Iraq
A man-made 32-foot and 60 tonne monument that is dates around 2000 BC is discovered in the Sea of Galilee
The new drug, ABT-450, with a 90-95% success rate for treating Hepatitis C, is announced

Melbourne captain Cameron Smith becomes highest point scorer in Australian Rugby League history; 49th minute penalty in Storm's 18-12 win over North Queensland in Townsville takes him to 2,420; Hazem El Masri, 2,418
Huge storm system produces more than 40 tornadoes in the US from Texas to South Carolina killing 32 people across six states
OPEC and other major oil companies agree to the largest-ever drop in production to stabilize world prices
Great Britain loosens its COVID-19 restrictions, opening pubs and shops after 175 days, the world's longest period of restrictions
Worst frost conditions in half a century will affect 80% of French vineyards according to industry officials
Global COVID-19 known cases pass 500 million, with 5.1 billion (66%) having received at least one vaccine shot
Terrorist attack on the New York subway injures 26, 10 by gunfire, with the suspect arrested a day later
Ghana is the first country to approve a new vaccine for malaria, Mosquirix (RTS,S), developed at Oxford University. It is the first major vaccine approved first in Africa.
New York City appoints it first-ever rat czar as Kathleen Corradi becomes the city's first-ever director of rat mitigation
US Environmental Protection Agency proposes landmark set of pollution regulations to drive down greenhouse-gas emissions, including electric vehicles making up 67% of new passenger-car sales by 2032
French KingFrancis Iorders the  Waldensian Protestants of the village of Mérindol to be punished, setting off a massacre
Richard Steele's British literary and society journal "The Tatler" is first published establishing a new type of journalism featuring essays on contemporary manners
French novelistGustave Flaubert's first novel and masterpiece "Madame Bovary" is published in book form
Confederate GeneralNathan Bedford Forrestcaptures Fort Pillow, Tennessee
Henrik Ibsen's play "Rosmersholm" premieres in Oslo
Alfred Hitchcockreleases his first film as director "The Pleasure Garden", in England; the silent drama was made in Germany
Kuomintang leaderChiang Kai-shekbegins a counter-revolution by violently suppressing Communist groups
Joe McCarthydebuts as New York Yankees manager
"Grand Hotel" directed by Edmund Goulding and starringGreta GarboandJohn Barrymorepremieres in New York, includes the line "I want to be alone" (Best Picture/Production 1932)
Vichy-France's head of government AdmiralFrançois Darlanconsults withAdolf Hitler
Lillian Hellman's "Searching Wind" premieres in NYC
Richard Strausscompletes his "Metamorphosen"
Bill Haley& His Comets record "Rock Around the Clock"
MLB Chicago White Sox ownerBill Veeckdebuts "Exploding Scoreboard" at Chicago's Comiskey Park
3rd Grammy Awards: Theme From a Summer Place,Ray Charleswins 4
Douglas MacArthurdeclines offer to become baseball commissioner
Yankees dedicate plaques toMickey Mantle&Joe DiMaggio
Anne Rice's debut novel "Interview with a Vampire" is published by Knopf
Australian film "Mad Max" starringMel Gibson, directed by George Miller is released
Canadian one-legged distance runnerTerry Foxbegins his "Marathon of Hope" attempt to cross the country at St. John's, Newfoundland
American composerRoger Sessionsreceives Pulitzer Prize for Music, for his "Concerto for Orchestra"
Harold Washingtonelected Chicago's 1st black mayor
Entertainer and restaurateurSonny Bonois elected mayor of Palm Springs, California
Frank Robinsonreplaces Cal Ripkin as manager of Baltimore Orioles
Peter Ueberrothdeal to purchase Eastern Airlines falls through
Anime TV series "Moomin", based on Moomin children's books byTove Jansson1st appears on Tokyo TV
James Brownmoves to a work-release center after serving 15 months of 6 year sentence for weapon and drug related convictions

Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include:Sting,Elton John,James Taylor,Jon Bon Jovi,Bruce Springsteen,Paul Simon,Billy Joel,Jessye Norman, and Geoffrey Oryema
Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include:Sting,Elton John,James Taylor, Don Henley,Diana Ross,Robin Williams, Branford Marsalis, and Kenny Kirkland
US PresidentBill Clintonis cited for contempt of court for giving "intentionally false statements" in a sexual harassment civil lawsuit.
Pedro Carmona becomes interim President of Venezuela during the military coup againstHugo Chávez.
PresidentMahmoud Abbasof the Palestinian National Authority makes a courtesy phone call to Prime MinisterBenjamin Netanyahuof Israel, restarting the Palestinian-Israeli dialogue
Hillary Clintonannounces she will run for the Democratic nominee for US President for the 2nd time
Breakthrough Starshot: Scientists and internet entrepreneurs, including Yuri Milner,Stephen HawkingandMark Zuckerberg, announce interstellar project to send robot spacecraft to Alpha Centauri
Pope Franciscalls for the world to be united in the face of COVID-19 as he delivers his Easter message from an empty St Peter's Cathedral in Rome
UK Prime MinisterBoris Johnsonapologies and is fined for attending parties during COVID-19 lockdown - first premier to be sanctioned for breaking the law
US PresidentJoe Bidenpays a visit to Northern Ireland to mark the 25th anniversary of the Good Friday Agreement
Music festival Coachella kicks off, headlined by Lana Del Ray, Tyler The Creator,Doja Catand No Doubt

Battle at Les Saintes: British fleet under Admiral George Rodney defeats the French fleet underComte de Grasseoff Dominica in the West Indies. Prevents a planned French and Spanish invasion of Jamaica.
Fort Sumter in South Carolina is attacked by the Confederacy, beginning the American Civil War
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltdies in office and Vice President Harry Truman is sworn in as 33rd US President
Leading icefall party of the British expedition to climb Mount Everest reaches Base Camp at 17,900 ft (5455 m)
Polio vaccine tested byJonas Salkannounced to be 'safe and effective' and is given full approval by the US Food and Drug Administration
Soviet cosmonautYuri Gagarinbecomes the first human to travel into space and orbit Earth, aboard the Vostok 1 spacecraft
Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara of Jainism, born in the Nāya Republic (d. 527 BC) [dates disputed]
En'yū, 64th Emperor of Japan (969-84), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 991)
Antonio da Sangallo the Younger, Italian architect, born in Florence (d. 1546)
Joachim Camerarius [Liebhard Kammerer], German classical scholar, born in Bamberg, Germany (d. 1574)
Marc-Antoine de Muret, French humanist, born in Muret, France (d. 1585)
Inca Garcilaso de la Vega, Peruvian writer and historian, born in Cusco, Castile (d.1616)
Edward de Vere, 17th Earl of Oxford, Lord Great Chamberlain of England, born in Hedingham Castle, Essex, England (d. 1604)
Christian IV of Denmark, King of Denmark and Norway (1588-1648), born in Frederiksborg Palace, Denmark (d. 1648)
François de Bassompierre, marshal of France, born in Haroué, France (d. 1646)
Paul Hainlein, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1686)
Caspar Burman, Dutch historian and Mayor of Utrecht, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1755)
William Cookworthy, English chemist, born in Kingsbridge, Devon (d. 1780)
Caffarelli [Gaetano Majorano], Italian castrato soprano, born in Bitonto, Kingdom of Naples, Habsburg Empire (d. 1783)
Guillaume Thomas François Raynal, French writer, born in Lapanouse, France (d. 1796)
Felice Giardini, Italian composer, born in Turin, Duchy of Savoy (d. 1796)
Pietro Nardini, Italian violinist and composer, born in Livorno, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1793)
Lyman Hall, American physician and signer (Declaration of Independence), born in Wallingford, Connecticut (d. 1790)
Charles Burney, English music historian, born in Shrewsbury, England (d. 1814)
Gaspare Gabellone, Italian composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples, Habsburg Empire (d. 1796)
Antoine Laurent de Jussieu, French botanist (Classification of flowering plants), born in Lyon, France (d. 1836)
Juan Manuel Olivares, Venezuelan composer, born in Caracas, Venezuela (d. 1797)
Dirk van den Boetzelaer, Regent of Holland, born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1819)
Giovanni Agostino Perotti, Italian composer, born in Vercelli, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1855)
Johan Erik Nordblom, Swedish composer, born in Uppsala, Sweden (d. 1848)
Francis Preston Blair, American journalist and newspaper editor (Washington Globe), born in Abingdon, Virginia (d. 1876)
John George Lambton, British statesman (1st Earl of Durham), born in London, England (d. 1840)
Germinal Pierre Dandelin, Belgian mathematician (geometry), born in Paris (d. 1847)
George N. Briggs, American lawyer and politician, 19th Governor of Massachusetts, born in Adams, Massachusetts (d. 1861)
Henri Druey, Swiss politician and a founding father of constitutional democracy in Switzerland, born in Faoug, Switzerland (d. 1855)
Henry de Cock, Dutch reformed vicar and secession leader, born in Veendam, Groningen, the Netherlands (d. 1842)
Joseph Franz Karl Lanner, Austrian composer and violinist, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1843)
Francis Libermann, French religious leader (Congregation of Heart), born in Saverne, France (d. 1852)
Henry Hugo Pierson, English composer, born in Oxford, England (d. 1873)
Edward Mollenhauer, Prussian-American violinist and composer (Love Among the Breakers), born in Erfurt, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1914)
Constantin Meunier, Belgian painter and sculptor, born in Etterbeek, Belgium (d. 1905)
George B. Anderson, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Hillsborough, North Carolina (d. 1862)
John Shaw Billings, American librarian and surgeon, born in Allensville, Switzerland County, Indiana (d. 1913)
Nikolay Przhevalsky, Russian naturalist and explorer (East and Central Asia), born in Kimborovo, Russia (d. 1888) (OS 31 March)
Victorin de Joncières, French composer (Dimitiri), and music critic, born in Paris (d. 1903)
Edmond Audran, French composer, born in Lyon, France (d. 1901)
Franz Xaver Haberl, German priest and musicologist (Magister choralis), born in Mallersdorf-Pfaffenberg, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1910)
José Gautier Benítez, Puerto Rican poet, born in Caguas, Puerto Rico (d. 1880)
Emil Liebling, German-American composer, born in Pless, Silesia (d. 1914)
Ferdinand von Lindemann, German mathematician (Proving π ["pi"] is a transcendental number), born in Hanover, German Confederation (now Germany) (d. 1939)
William Conway, 1st Baron Conway of Allington, English historian and explorer (Spitzbergen), born in Rochester, Kent (d. 1937)
Raul Pompeia, Brazilian writer (O Ateneu), born in Angra dos Reis, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 1895)
Akiyama Saneyuki, Japanese officer in the Imperial Japanese Navy, born in Matsuyama, Iyo Province, Japan (d. 1918)
Henri Désiré Landru, French sex murderer, born in Paris (d. 1922)
Ioannis Metaxas, Greek military officer, politician, and dictator (1936-41), born in Ithaca, Greece (d. 1941)

Vic Willis, American Baseball HOF pitcher (no-hitter 1899; World Series 1909; MLB ERA leader 1899 & strikeout leader 1902; Boston Beaneaters, Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Cecil County, Maryland (d. 1947)
Richard B Goldschmidt, German-American zoologist (butterflies), born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany (d. 1958)
Frederic G. Melcher, American publisher and editor who founded children's book week, born in Malden, Massachusetts (d. 1963)

Addie Joss, American Baseball HOF pitcher (perfect game 1908; no hitters 1908, 10; 1.89 career ERA 2nd-lowest in MLB history; career WHIP 0.968 MLB record; Cleveland Bronchos), born in Woodland, Wisconsin (d. 1911)
Dally Messenger, Australian rugby league centre (9 Tests; NSW; Eastern Suburbs; ARL 'Immortal'; Dally M Medal) and rugby union utility (2 caps), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1959)
Imogen Cunningham, American photographer (1965 ASMP award), born in Portland, Oregon (d. 1976)
Otto Meyerhof, German psychologist and biochemist (Nobel 1922), born in Hannover, Germany (d. 1951)
Tenby Davies, Welsh athlete (world half-mile professional champion 1909), born in Tenby, Pembrokeshire, Wales (d. 1932)
R. D. Banerji, Indian archaeologist and historian (discovered ancient Indus city of Mohenjo-daro), born in Berhampore, British India (d. 1930)[1]
Robert Delaunay, French artist, born in Paris, France (d. 1941)
Harold Lockwood, American actor (Tess of the Storm Country), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1918)
Dan Ahearn, Irish-American athlete (men's triple jump WR 15.52m 1911-24), born in Athea, Ireland (d. 1942)
Heinrich Neuhaus, Soviet pianist (The Art of Piano Playing), born in Elisavetgrad, Russian Empire (d. 1964)
Johnny Dodds, American early jazz clarinetist, born in Waveland, Mississippi (d. 1940)
Francisco Craveiro Lopes, 12th President of Portugal (1951-58), born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1964)
Fred Barratt, English cricket fast bowler (5 Tests, 5 wickets; Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Annesley, England (d. 1947)
(Alice) "Lily" Pons, French-American coloratura soprano opera singer (Metropolitan Opera, 1931-60) and actress (That Girl From Paris), born in Draguignan, France (d. 1976)
Chief Thundercloud [Victor Daniels], Cherokee actor (The Lone Ranger; Colt .45), born in Muskogee, Indian Territory (d. 1955)
Lowell Stockman, American politician, U.S. House of Representatives from Oregon, born in Helix, Oregon (d. 1962)
Louis J. M. Beel, Prime Minister of Netherlands (1946-48, 58-59), born in Roermond, Netherlands (d. 1977)
Jan Tinbergen, Dutch economist (Plan of Labor, Nobel 1969), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Frankie Masters [Masterman], American banjo player, guitarist, vocalist and big band bandleader ("Scatter-Brain"), born in St. Marys, West Virginia (d. 1990)
Harald Penrose, British test pilot, born in Hereford, England (d. 1996)
Felix de Weldon, Austrian-American sculptor (Marine Corps War Memorial), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 2003)
Hardie Gramatky, American author and animator (Little Toot), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1979)
Carlos Lleras Restrepo, President of Colombia (1966-70), born in Bogotá, Colombia (d. 1994)
Robert Lee Scott, Jr., American General and Air Force pilot, born in Waynesboro, Georgia (d. 2006)
Virginia Cherrill, American actress (City Lights; Lake Extra; Brat), born in Santa Barbara, California (d. 1996)
Jürgen Rausch, German philosopher and author (End of the Playboys), born in Bremen, Germany (d. 1995)
Darrell A. Amyx, American classical archaeologist (Greek Ceramics), born in Exeter, California (d. 1997)
Geoff Chubb, South African cricket fast bowler (5 Tests, 21 wickets; Border), born in East London, Eastern Cape, South Africa (d. 1982)
Mahmoud Younis, Suez Canal nationalization engineer, born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1976)
Eric Feldary, Hungarian actor (Hold Back the Dawn; 16 Fathoms Deep), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1968)
Georges Franju, French film director (Judex; Les Yeux sans visage - Eyes Without A Face), born in Fougères, Ille-et-Vilaine, France (d. 1987)
Harold Maguire, British air marshal, born in Kilkishen, Ireland (d. 2001)
Walter "Walt" Gorney, American character actor (Friday the 13th; Trading Places), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2004)
Gabor Jodal, Romanian composer, born in Székelyudvarhely (d. 1989)
Keiko Fukuda, Japanese-born American martial artist, born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 2013)
Armen Alchian, American economist and professor (University of California), born in Fresno, California (d. 2013)
Emil Petaja, American sci-fi author (Star Mill, Tramontane), born in Milltown, Missoula County, Montana (d. 2000)
Ernest Borneman, German sexual researcher, psychotherapist, musician and writer (Bang, You're Dead), born in Berlin (d. 1995)
Hound Dog Taylor [Theodore Roosevelt], American Chicago blues guitarist and singer, born in Natchez, Mississippi (d. 1975)
Benjamin Libet, American scientist (human consciousness), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2007)
Beverly Cleary, American Newberry Medal winning writer of children's and young adult fiction (Ramona series, 1955-99), born in McMinnville, Oregon (d. 2021)
Brian Connell, British writer and broadcaster, born in Hertford (d. 1999)
Helen Forrest [Fogel], American big band, pop, and swing jazz singer (Artie Shaw; Benny Goodman; Harry James), born in Atlantic City, New Jersey (d. 1999)[1]
M Marie Wadlow, American softball pitcher (Hall of Fame 1957), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1979)
Vinoo Mankad, Indian cricket all-rounder (44 Tests, 5 x 100, HS 231, 162 wickets, BB 8/52; Nawanagar, Hindus, Gujarat, Bombay, Rajasthan), born in Jamnagar, India (d. 1978)
Billy Vaughn, American singer, saxophonist, and bandleader ("Sail Along Sil'vry Moon"), born in Glasgow, Kentucky (d. 1991)

István Anhalt, Hungarian-Canadian classical and electronic music composer (La Tourangelle; Winthrop), educator (McGill; Queens (Ontario) Universities), and writer (Alternative Voices), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2012)[1]
Maurice Girodias, French publisher and founder (Olympia Press), born in Paris (d. 1990)
Robert Fizdale, American pianist (Misia), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1995)
Alfred Mabbs, British archivist (Public Records Office), born in London (d. 2009)
(Edwin) "Ted" Astley, British arranger, bandleader, and film and television composer (The Mouse That Roared; The Saint), born in Warrington, Lancashire, England (d. 1998)
Ann Miller [Johnnie Lucille Collier], American actress, dancer, and singer (On the Town), born in Chireno, Texas (d. 2004)
Curtis Turner, American auto racer (184 NASCAR races over 17 years, 17 wins), born in Floyd, Virginia (d. 1970)
Julius Moormann, Dutch student and resistance fighter (WWII), born in Nijmegen, Netherlands (d. 1944)
Peter Safar, Austrian physician (pioneered cardiopulmonary resuscitation or CPR), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2003)
Sergiu Natra [Nadler], Romanian-Israeli composer (Symphony in Red, Blue, Yellow and Green), and music professor (Tel Aviv University, 1975-85), born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 2021)
Evelyn Berezin, American computer pioneer, invented first word processor, born in the Bronx, New York (d. 2018)
Johnny Jordan, English cameraman (You Only Live Twice), born in Oxfordshire, England (d. 1969)
Oliver Postgate, English animator, puppeteer and writer, born in Hendon, London (d. 2008)
James Hillman, American psychologist, and educator (C.G. Jung Institute, Zurich), born in Atlantic City, New Jersey (d. 2011)
Jane Withers, American film child star (Bright Eyes; Ginger), character actress (Giant), and commercial spokesperson (Josephine the Plumber), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 2021)
Alvin Sargent, American Academy Award-winning screenwriter for "Julia" (1977) and "Ordinary People" (1980), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2019)
Patrick Meehan, Scottish petty criminal, wrongly convicted of murder and later given a royal pardon, born in Scotland (d. 1994)[1]
Brooklyn Supreme, Belgian stallion and once the heaviest known horse at 1,450 kg (3,200 lb), born in Minneapolis (d. 1948)[1]
Hardy Krüger, German actor (The Flight of the Phoenix), novelist, and travel writer, born in Wedding, Berlin, Germany (d. 2022)
Jean-François Paillard, French conductor, and arranger (Pachelbel's Canon in D), born in Vitry-le-François, France (d. 2013)
Russell L. Rogers, American electrical engineer and astronaut (X-20), born in Lawrence, Kansas (d. 1967)
Uwe Kitzinger, German-British political scientist, educator and administrator (President of Templeton College Oxford), born in Nuremberg, Germany (d. 2023)
Elspet Gray, Lady Rix, Scottish actress (4 Weddings & a Funeral, Solo, Tenko), born in Inverness, Inverness-shire, Scotland (d. 2013)
Betty Clooney, American singer (Jack Paar Show), born in Maysville, Kentucky (d. 1976)
Bryan Magee, British philosopher, politician (Member of Parliament, 1974-83), writer (Modern British Philosophy), and broadcaster (Thinking Aloud), born in Hoxton, London (d. 2019)
